metadata.title: Rolling Correlations — Normal vs Crisis Regimes with Claude
metadata.note: CLI video spine: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06 slate · B07 slate · B08 slate · B09 outro

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

Diversification rests on one assumption: that assets do not all move together. In normal markets, correlations between stocks are modest — perhaps 0.3. But in a crisis, correlations spike toward 1. Diversification fails exactly when you need it most. Today we build rolling correlation analysis with Claude, inject a simulated crisis period, and visualize the regime shift in a correlation heatmap.
beats.visual_intent: title card: correlation matrix morphing from low-corr (golden) to high-corr (crimson)

beats.narration_text: Type the prompt. Generate 5 years of synthetic daily returns for 5 assets with off-diagonal correlations of 0.3. Inject 30 crisis days where correlations spike to 0.85. Compute 60-day rolling correlations for all 10 pairs. Print average correlation in normal versus crisis periods.

props.command:
claude "Write rolling_correlation.py: generate 5 years synthetic daily
returns for 5 assets with off-diagonal correlations ~0.3; inject
30-day crisis where correlations spike to 0.85; compute 60-day
rolling correlations for all 10 pairs; print average correlation:
normal vs crisis periods."

beats.narration_text: Read it. The correlated returns are generated by Cholesky decomposition: start with a correlation matrix, take its Cholesky factor L, multiply L by a vector of independent standard normals. The result is a multivariate normal vector with the target correlation structure. During the crisis window, swap in a different correlation matrix with off-diagonal correlations of 0.85.

props.code:
# rolling_correlation.py  —  Claude Code output
import numpy as np, pandas as pd
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
# Normal correlation matrix (rho=0.3)
def make_cov(rho, n=5):
    C = np.full((n,n), rho)
    np.fill_diagonal(C, 1.0)
    return C
L_norm = np.linalg.cholesky(make_cov(0.30))
L_crisis = np.linalg.cholesky(make_cov(0.85))
# Generate returns: Cholesky trick
Z = rng.normal(0, 1, (1260, 5))  # 5 years
R = (Z @ L_norm.T) * 0.01        # sigma=1%%

beats.narration_text: Run it. The heatmap shows the 5-by-5 correlation matrix — diagonal always 1.0 in crimson, off-diagonal in gold at 0.30 in the normal regime. The transition shows what happens during the crisis: the off-diagonal cells turn crimson as correlations spike to 0.85. The message is stark: every asset pair moves together.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: 5x5 correlation heatmap morphing from golden (normal, 0.30) to crimson (crisis, 0.85)

beats.narration_text: Rolling correlations are noisy on a 60-day window. What if you use a longer 120-day window? The signal persists but the crisis spike is less sharp. Ask Claude to compare 60-day and 120-day rolling correlations and show which window better detects the crisis onset.

props.command:
claude "update rolling_correlation.py:
  -> compare 60-day and 120-day rolling correlation windows
  -> plot both rolling series for one asset pair
  -> measure: days to detect crisis onset at rho > 0.6"

beats.narration_text: The 60-day window detects the crisis 8 days faster than the 120-day window. But it generates 30 percent more false alarms in normal periods. There is no free lunch: a shorter window is more reactive but noisier. In practice, risk managers use EWMA — exponentially weighted moving average — as a compromise: more weight on recent observations without the hard cutoff.
beats.visual_intent: summary card: 60d vs 120d rolling correlation time series; crisis detection lag

beats.narration_text: Your move: implement EWMA correlations. The RiskMetrics model uses a decay factor of 0.94 for daily data — each day's observation is 94 percent as important as the previous one. Compute the EWMA covariance matrix and compare it to the 60-day rolling version.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: EWMA formula, decay factor 0.94, RiskMetrics reference

beats.narration_text: Then use the crisis-period correlation matrix as a stress scenario. If the real portfolio had 5 assets at 0.3 average correlation and you stress to 0.85, by how much does portfolio VaR increase? That is a standard regulatory stress test.
beats.visual_intent: code snippet: stress test portfolio VaR with crisis correlation matrix

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
